subject: reading
prompt:

passage: *Life Among the Piutes* is an 1883 autobiographical narrative by Sarah Winnemucca Hopkins. In the work, Winnemucca creates suspense by emphasizing her physical response to an event, writing ___
questionText: Which quotation from *Life Among the Piutes* most effectively illustrates the claim?

choices.text: "So ended our feast, and every family went to its own home in the pine-nut mountains, and remained there till the pine-nuts were ripe. They ripen about the last of June."

"Oh, how my heart jumped when I heard a noise close by. It was a horse running towards us. We had to lie down close to the ground. It came close to us and stopped. Oh, how my heart beat! I thought whoever it was would hear my heart beat."
"The day we were to start we partook of the first gathering of food for that summer. So that morning everybody prayed, and sang songs, and danced, and ate before starting."

"Late in that fall, there came news that my grandfather was on his way home. Then my father took a great many of his men and went to meet his father, and there came back a runner, saying, that all our people must come together."

But researchers Marisol Vega and Nathan McKee have pointed out that "invasive" and "native" are labels that describe temporary circumstances. In the case of Maryland, these observations suggest that ______

choices.text: it's useful at present for the state to distinguish between invasive and native species in some instances but not in the case of the flathead catfish.
the state was previously home to some flathead catfish but they were outcompeted by invading species.
the state's designation of the flathead catfish as invasive may be appropriate now but not in the future.
even if Earth's climate doesn't change in the way scientists predict, the flathead catfish will likely establish itself in the state.

companies did not benefit from liberalization until at least three years after liberalization occurred.

companies in Venezuela experienced a greater increase in investment following liberalization than did companies in Indonesia.

prompt:
What is the main topic of the text?
The following text is from John Manuel Arias's 2023 novel *Where There Was Fire*. Mariana lives in Barrio Esperanza, a neighborhood in a Costa Rican city. Observant clouds gathered over Barrio Esperanza. Mariana sat up in her bed, startled by a flashing news report on television. She wasn't sure when she'd fallen asleep. Four in the morning, maybe. On most nights, sleep came quickly to her. Violently. But last night, Mariana felt as if she had waded into it slowly, almost willingly. As one does when playful moonlight splashes in the receding tide and invites you to join it.

subject: reading
prompt:
Which finding, if true, would most directly support Romero and colleagues' conclusion?
Southwestern Nevada's Esmeralda County is among the most rural counties in the United States: the US Census Bureau classified it as 100% rural in 2010. Researchers have struggled to recruit residents of counties like Esmeralda for inclusion in studies. Noting this problem, Daniela Romero and colleagues tested two recruitment techniques in rural counties: purposive sampling, in which researchers contact many potential participants who have the characteristics desired for a given study, and snowball sampling, in which researchers recruit a few people with the desired characteristics and ask them to recruit additional participants. Romero and colleagues concluded that the latter approach may be superior.
passage: Southwestern Nevada's Esmeralda County is among the most rural counties in the United States: the US Census Bureau classified it as 100% rural in 2010. Researchers have struggled to recruit residents of counties like Esmeralda for inclusion in studies. Noting this problem, Daniela Romero and colleagues tested two recruitment techniques in rural counties: purposive sampling, in which researchers contact many potential participants who have the characteristics desired for a given study, and snowball sampling, in which researchers recruit a few people with the desired characteristics and ask them to recruit additional participants. Romero and colleagues concluded that the latter approach may be superior.
questionText: Which finding, if true, would most directly support Romero and colleagues' conclusion?

choices.text: Most participants recruited through snowball sampling had different characteristics than the initial participants who recruited them.
Participants recruited through snowball sampling were more likely to have taken part in previous studies than were participants recruited through purposive sampling.
Snowball sampling yielded significantly more participants with the desired characteristics than purposive sampling did.
Snowball sampling yielded significantly more participants than would be expected if researchers contacted people without regard to those people's characteristics.

subject: reading
prompt:
Based on the texts, how would Lorde (Text 1) most likely respond to the argument presented in Text 2?
Text 1
The poet Audre Lorde once claimed that poetry is the most inexpensive of art forms to practice. While people who pursue other art forms—sculpture, film, long-form prose—require large blocks of uninterrupted time as well as money to complete their work, poets can write, as Lorde said, "between shifts, in the hospital pantry, on the subway, and on scraps of surplus paper." So poets can practice their art even if they must earn their living in another way.
Text 2
Any assessment of the state of contemporary poetry must reckon with the professionalization of the field. While it is possible in theory for anyone to publish in *AGNI*, *Ploughshares*, or a similar major poetry outlet, many people who do so have professional training in poetry and extensive practice writing it, which requires time not often available to those who must also work full-time jobs. Thus, financial security indirectly affects which people become poets.
passage:
Text 1
The poet Audre Lorde once claimed that poetry is the most inexpensive of art forms to practice. While people who pursue other art forms—sculpture, film, long-form prose—require large blocks of uninterrupted time as well as money to complete their work, poets can write, as Lorde said, "between shifts, in the hospital pantry, on the subway, and on scraps of surplus paper." So poets can practice their art even if they must earn their living in another way.
Text 2
Any assessment of the state of contemporary poetry must reckon with the professionalization of the field. While it is possible in theory for anyone to publish in *AGNI*, *Ploughshares*, or a similar major poetry outlet, many people who do so have professional training in poetry and extensive practice writing it, which requires time not often available to those who must also work full-time jobs. Thus, financial security indirectly affects which people become poets.
questionText: Based on the texts, how would Lorde (Text 1) most likely respond to the argument presented in Text 2?

choices.text: By indicating that those artists who specialize in more financially rewarding artistic forms are unlikely to also be successful as poets
By suggesting that those poets who publish in major poetry journals are most likely to be able to earn a living by writing poetry
By pointing out that people can gain experience writing poetry while also working full-time jobs
By mentioning that people often work full-time jobs in order to afford professional training in writing poetry

choices.text: "And Lady Caroline, sitting where [the others] had left her before breakfast on the wall, peeped over when she heard laughter, and saw them standing on the path below, and thought what a mercy it was they were laughing down there and had not come up and done it round her."

"The idea that [Lady Caroline] would go on giving orders was too absurd. She never gave orders at home. Nobody there dreamed of asking her to do anything. That such a very tiresome activity should be thrust upon her here, simply because she happened to be able to talk Italian, was ridiculous."

"'What does one do with people like this?' [Lady Caroline] asked herself, her eyes fixed on Mrs. Fisher in what felt to her an indignant stare but appeared to Mrs. Fisher as really charming docility."
"A deep cynicism took hold of the unhappy [Lady Caroline]. Her inside grew hoary with disillusionment, while her gracious and charming outside continued to make the world more beautiful."

subject: reading
prompt:
Which finding, if true, would most directly support Whitlock and Harmon's conclusion?
Sidebells wintergreen (*Orthilia secunda*) plants are native to Alaska, where harsh conditions have historically impeded potential invasive species. As the boreal climate has warmed in recent decades, however, common pepperweed (*Lepidium densiflorum*) plants have established themselves in Alaska. It has been suggested that warming-induced delays in the onset of subfreezing temperatures in autumn can benefit invasives more than native species; to evaluate this possibility, biologists Nora Whitlock and Elise Harmon tracked *O. secunda* and *L. densiflorum*, along with other native and invasive species, over several years, concluding that invasives are advantaged by delays in subfreezing temperature onset in Alaska.
passage: Sidebells wintergreen (*Orthilia secunda*) plants are native to Alaska, where harsh conditions have historically impeded potential invasive species. As the boreal climate has warmed in recent decades, however, common pepperweed (*Lepidium densiflorum*) plants have established themselves in Alaska. It has been suggested that warming-induced delays in the onset of subfreezing temperatures in autumn can benefit invasives more than native species; to evaluate this possibility, biologists Nora Whitlock and Elise Harmon tracked *O. secunda* and *L. densiflorum*, along with other native and invasive species, over several years, concluding that invasives are advantaged by delays in subfreezing temperature onset in Alaska.
questionText: Which finding, if true, would most directly support Whitlock and Harmon's conclusion?

choices.text: Although *O. secunda* and *L. densiflorum* tended to stop producing leaves at about the same time in years with historically typical temperature patterns, *L. densiflorum* stopped producing leaves sooner than *O. secunda* did in years with late subfreezing temperature onset.

Although *O. secunda* and *L. densiflorum* both tended to produce leaves later into autumn in years with late subfreezing temperature onset, the extension was much greater for *L. densiflorum* than for *O. secunda*.

Although *O. secunda* and *L. densiflorum* both tended to produce more leaves overall in years with late subfreezing temperature onset than they did in years with historically typical temperature patterns, the years with late subfreezing temperature onset also had early growing season onset in spring.

Although significant interannual variations in subfreezing temperature onset were observed during the study, neither *L. densiflorum* nor *O. secunda* showed any significant interannual variation in the cessation of leaf production.

passage: American Express's introduction of an airport lounge in 2013 is an instance of brand extension—the company leveraged its brand recognition as a financial services provider to enter a new product category. To understand consumer responses to extended brands, Nicole Hartley et al. identified 30 extended-brand pairs (e.g., the same brand of water bottle and running shoes) in household purchasing data and, for each pair, had consumers rate the similarity of the pair's product categories. Noting that cross-category purchasing of the same brand was higher in pairs of product categories rated as similar, such as bread and bagels, than in those rated as dissimilar, such as deodorant and baking soda, a student claims that similarity drives cross-category purchasing.
questionText: Which finding, if true, would most directly weaken the student's conclusion?

choices.text: Products in pairs with high similarity ratings tended to be among the most expensive products in their categories, whereas products in pairs with low similarity ratings tended to be among the least expensive products in their categories.

Consumers were more likely to purchase both products in pairs with similarity ratings comparable to those of bread and bagels than they were to purchase both products in pairs with similarity ratings comparable to those of deodorant and baking soda.

Pairs with high similarity ratings tended to be in categories in which there were relatively few competing products from other brands, whereas pairs with low similarity ratings tended to be in categories in which there were many competing products from other brands.
The positive correlation between similarity ratings and cross-category purchasing was equally strong for pairs with low brand recognition and pairs with high brand recognition.

subject: reading
prompt:
Which choice most logically completes the text?
Marissa Elena Byrne and colleagues had musicians and nonmusicians with clinically average hearing listen to recordings of nature sounds and of music in popular genres like classic rock and orchestral music, then adjust the volume to optimize their listening enjoyment. The researchers found that for a given recording that a musician and nonmusician identified as their favorite among those included in the study, optimal volume tended to be higher for the musician than for the nonmusician. Thus, if a musician and nonmusician both identified Led Zeppelin's "Whole Lotta Love" as their favorite recording and the musician preferred to listen to it at a volume of 84.8 decibels, the nonmusician would be expected to ______
passage: Marissa Elena Byrne and colleagues had musicians and nonmusicians with clinically average hearing listen to recordings of nature sounds and of music in popular genres like classic rock and orchestral music, then adjust the volume to optimize their listening enjoyment. The researchers found that for a given recording that a musician and nonmusician identified as their favorite among those included in the study, optimal volume tended to be higher for the musician than for the nonmusician. Thus, if a musician and nonmusician both identified Led Zeppelin's "Whole Lotta Love" as their favorite recording and the musician preferred to listen to it at a volume of 84.8 decibels, the nonmusician would be expected to ______
questionText: Which choice most logically completes the text?

choices.text: enjoy listening to the nature sounds at 84.8 decibels more than listening to "Whole Lotta Love" at 84.8 decibels.
not find it enjoyable to listen to any recordings at a volume as low as 84.8 decibels.
prefer listening to other music at 84.8 decibels over listening to "Whole Lotta Love" at that volume.
find listening to "Whole Lotta Love" at 84.8 decibels less enjoyable than listening to it at a lower volume.

subject: reading
prompt:
Which choice most logically completes the text?
All stainless steel contains varying amounts of iron, carbon, and corrosion-inhibiting chromium. However, ferritic stainless steel, often used for cooking utensils, contains a higher percentage of chromium (at least 10.5%) than does austenitic stainless steel and a higher concentration of iron, which is responsible for its magnetic properties. Unlike ferritic stainless steel, austenitic stainless steel has a face-centered cubic crystalline structure resulting from the addition of nickel to the alloy. Austenitic stainless steel has two subtypes: the 200 series, often used for washing machines, and the 300 series, which has more nickel than the 200 series and is often used for mining and chemical equipment or sinks. Thus, stainless steel used to manufacture ______
passage: All stainless steel contains varying amounts of iron, carbon, and corrosion-inhibiting chromium. However, ferritic stainless steel, often used for cooking utensils, contains a higher percentage of chromium (at least 10.5%) than does austenitic stainless steel and a higher concentration of iron, which is responsible for its magnetic properties. Unlike ferritic stainless steel, austenitic stainless steel has a face-centered cubic crystalline structure resulting from the addition of nickel to the alloy. Austenitic stainless steel has two subtypes: the 200 series, often used for washing machines, and the 300 series, which has more nickel than the 200 series and is often used for mining and chemical equipment or sinks. Thus, stainless steel used to manufacture ______
questionText: Which choice most logically completes the text?

choices.text: cooking utensils will have a face-centered cubic crystalline structure.
mining and chemical equipment will have a concentration of chromium lower than 10.5%, while stainless steel used to manufacture washing machines will not.
washing machines will have a lower percentage of chromium in its composition than stainless steel used for cooking utensils will.
sinks will have similar magnetic properties to stainless steel used to manufacture cooking utensils.
